speaker: interviewer
text: Interview with Moses Kaufman. This is tape number seven. Moses, you were telling us that you had went to Bialystock to work in a tannery there. How did you get to Bialystock?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was like this. When I was released from my position in the tanneries, working for the Red Army, as I mentioned that my boss was not-- was the minister to light-- from light-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] from the light industry, Mr. [INAUDIBLE]. They arranged for me there [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a truck where my-- that I and my wife, some of my belongings-- even a piano that I got up-- got back-- find out who the piano was taken away by a Lithuanian when the Russian came in. And everything was loaded on the truck, and the truck from the army was bringing me to Bialystock. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And I know also that Lublin was liberated through the-- from the-- of the Germans, liberated through the Russian Army. So when I came to Bialystock, I ask that governor, Mr. [INAUDIBLE], to allow me to go to Lublin, because Lublin wasn't far from Radom, and I'm very anxious to find out what happens to my father and my family. And I promise him I didn't-- that I would come back to him. Came to Lublin, and so I went to Lublin. In Lublin, the government [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] find out that I am there, and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I told them about this, that I have to go back to Bialystock. Radom wasn't yet liberated-- that I would have to go back to Bialystock because I promise that [INAUDIBLE] that I am coming, that-- They said, no, you are not going back there, because we need you [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] in Gdansk, in Danzig, where there was some tanneries there, and that you would have to put in motion the tanneries over there, and we are sending you over there. They send me over there from Lublin. I went there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did Ala come with you to Lublin?

speaker: interviewee
text: To Lublin? She come to Lublin, of course, from Bialystock.

speaker: interviewer
text: So did you ever go back to Bialystock?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, but the only one explanation is that I have been under the orders from the [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] government people that I have to go over there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how long did you spend in Lublin altogether?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Lublin I spent only a few weeks-- a few weeks. In the meantime, I find my sister because Auschwitz was liberated, and one of my best friends was the secretary of the Jewish committee. And he was always, when some places were liberated, and especially camps were liberated, so he got admission to find out who's at-- who-- where from they are coming and to organize everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he told me the story that what happens in Krakow, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that he was organizing the people there from Auschwitz that were liberated, you know? And he told me that he was asking always who was from that town, from that town, from that town, from that town. And he asked who is from Vilna? So my sister-- that's what he told me. I'm repeating what he told me. So my sister went up to him and said, oh, Vilna, Vilna, I have a brother there. He was working in a tannery. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And-- what his name? He asked, what his name? She said Kaufman.

speaker: interviewee
text: But your name's Kasman. No, that's from my [INAUDIBLE], a marriage to a Mr. Kasman. So he said, please, if you don't mind-- he didn't want-- because he knows me very well-- please sit down. I will come to you. He finished off with the whole [? registration ?] and so on, so called her. Mrs. Kasman, what you told me, you told me about-- about Kaufman, a brother Kaufman, that was working in tanneries. She said, yes, yes, yes. Hm. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Tomorrow I am arranging that you will be in that and that flat-- the address he gave her, [INAUDIBLE] with my cousin, one of my cousins. Your brother will pick you up tomorrow from there. I took from Lublin. He came and told me the whole story. He gave me the address where he is, where he find her, after two and 1/2 years being in Auschwitz. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And [pauses] he gave me the address. I took a Jeep with one of the Russian officers. Went-- the war was going on. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I went through [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] places that, from both sides, they were shooting, and everything was in fire, you know. It was about 5:00 in the morning, I think, to Krakow-- from Lublin to Krakow-- got the address. She opened the door, and that was when I-- when I was find my sister. Being in Lublin. I'm talking about the time when I was in Lublin.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was that, to find your sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was-- what I can tell you? I didn't recognize her.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because after so many years, to be over two years-- two and 1/2 years in-- in-- in Auschwitz. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] She was sent there with a court order in 1940. No, '40-- sorry. 1941, if I don't-- I would like to make it-- I can't tell you exactly. With a court order as a political prisoner to Auschwitz. Auschwitz was built as a prisoner. To [INAUDIBLE] not as a Jewess, a prisoner, as a-- as a-- no, how to call this. As a-- [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] she was sent with a court order there. So the selections, because she was registered in the Red Cross in Switzerland. That order, that court order was written.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was she a political--

speaker: interviewee
text: She was tre-- as a political-- as a political-- as a political-- how to call this? A political prisoner. And she did selections. She did learn for selection in German. [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: So after you found-- after you found your sister in Krakow--

speaker: interviewee
text: What?

speaker: interviewer
text: After you found your sister in Krakow, where did you go from there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Lublin.

speaker: interviewer
text: With her?

speaker: interviewee
text: With her.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long did you stay in Lublin for?

speaker: interviewee
text: Only a few weeks. Only two weeks or something like that. And after, I got the orders to go to Gdansk. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So we went all to Gdansk.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you do there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Putting in motion all the tanneries from [INAUDIBLE] down to [INAUDIBLE]. All pre-Baltic-- all pre-Baltic-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the all-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Baltic is the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Baltic states.

speaker: interviewee
text: Baltic states, you know? The old Baltic states [INAUDIBLE]. And [INAUDIBLE] I put in motion-- I put a part of the [INAUDIBLE]. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Also a few tanneries there that I put in motion.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long did you spend in Gdansk?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not long.

speaker: interviewer
text: Not long?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not long, because my mind was Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: And--

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was working already at the time in the Bricha. [? Yichud, ?] sorry-- [? Yichud. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: What's Yichud?

speaker: interviewee
text: They're all to work for illegally to go to Palestine, [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: And was Ala with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ala was with me.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who else was with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hella.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did Hella have-- was she married?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hella was married, but her husband was-- I have to go back a little bit. [LAUGHS] The husband, her [? Jirik, ?] [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] when the war start, he escape, and he was in Lvov. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] At three months before the war start with Germany and Russia, the Russian authorities asked everyone leaving-- they called this [NON-ENGLISH] On the demarcation lane, close the demarcation line, on the River Bug, that everyone could take up the Russian citizenship. Some took up the Russian [? citizenship. ?] Most of them didn't took up the Russian citizenship. So they went-- sent all to Siberia, and they were-- they know how to do this. Amongst them was my-- my-- my brother in law.

speaker: interviewer
text: So was-- who was living with you? Who were the people living with you at the time in Gdansk?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Gdansk? Hella, my sister, my wife, my sister, and my son was born there.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name is Ian Kaufman. Dr. Ian Kaufman.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was he born?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was born in 1945, in Danzig.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what month was he born?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was born in June-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] September, sorry.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the war was already over.

speaker: interviewee
text: The war was over.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you when you found out the war was over?

speaker: interviewee
text: When the war was over, I was still in Gdansk, at part, only very shortly, in Radom. Actually, I was that time in Radom, when the war-- [INAUDIBLE] 1945 when the war was--

speaker: interviewer
text: What were you doing in Radom?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Radom, unfortunately, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was trying to take in possession back the tannery, my father's tannery. And I spent [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a good three months or more, even, a four months, trying [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] through the court. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Beforehand the old institutions, what has got, because that was all still [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] half nationalized, you know? Nationalized, I would say. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That they are not against that I am getting back the factory, my father's factory. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So I have to go through the court, even, too. And I got papers here stating to bring me in as the owner of my father's factory. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And a few [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] days later, when I got out from the [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] court, that I got out to put me back in possession of the-- every factory that introduced-- that was a new law that every factory what is engaging more than 40 people has to be nationalized. So I have been left with the paper, and the factory is there. And did you find out what happened to your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. That was the first day that I came to Radom, when Radom was liberated. A few days after the li-- the Radom was liberated, I came to Radom. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The first thing what I was trying to do-- couldn't see any Jews-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to go to the cemetery, because my mother, my brother, was buried there, to make a visit to my mother, my brother. [SIGHS] The cemetery was about four, five kilometers from the town. I was walking four kilometers, six kilometers, three kilometers, backwards and forwards. I couldn't find the place. And I know exactly where the cemetery was, because that was a big-- Jews were living about 500 years there in-- in Radom. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So I couldn't find the place. And actually, the cemetery was with a fence, a colossal fence from red bricks, the whole cemetery was-- I couldn't find even not a brick there. So I went into one of the villages-- villages to find out, where is the Jewish cemetery [INAUDIBLE]? I went out. A villager came out, and I asked him, listen, could you tell me where the Jewish cemetery is? And as I was turned a little bit, he-- he just sinked in the air. I couldn't find him anymore. So I start to look after him. And what I find? Instead to find him, I find a shed, a colossal shed, about 20 meters long, and about [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] two and 1/2 meters high, full of tombs-- tombs-- Tombstones.

speaker: interviewee
text: Tomb-- tombstones [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] from the cemetery, one after the other. Full. Mind you, I want to tell you, it wasn't done through the Germans. It was done through the Poles, through the municipality of Poles and through the Poles, through the people and Poles what are living next to the--

speaker: interviewer
text: You were telling me that you-- about your father, what happened to him.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the first day-- I'm coming back to it. The first day when I came to Radom, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I have in mind a visit to my mother's tombs. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I was walking, and everything was, for me, you know, very-- I was very upset and so on. And walking on the [NON-ENGLISH], and a [NON-ENGLISH], a policeman, a Jewish boy-- he wasn't looking Jewish at all, with the the lea-- with the arm band, the red one. He came to me. He said, I know you. You are Mr. Kaufman's son. I was burying your father. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: I asked him, that was for me? Just imagine how that-- you can imagine what-- what-- what-- what kind of a feeling I got and so I asked him, please tell me-- you are telling me something. Yes. I was burying not only your father, but other-- that was 18 people that was on the third-- the second or the third-- third May, that he was burying him.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he told me that was [INAUDIBLE], a place not far from Radom. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] That they were shot there, singly, and he was buried them, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] nine people after a paper with bitumen-- bitumen paper or something like that, and after another paper, and buried them there.

speaker: interviewer
text: I asked him, could you show me that place? I took a carriage, a [NON-ENGLISH], they called this, and I went over there with him. He's showing me the place. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I noticed-- so I start to do some-- I was trying to do the exhumation, and to-- but where? I didn't know where the-- what to do, you know, or to exhumate, but where to bury?

speaker: interviewee
text: So-- I came the second time there to find out if-- because I notice that it's flat, you know, because there are some other graves from Russian P-- [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: Prisoners of war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Prisoners of war, prisoners of war, 32 in every-- in every grave was, and that was still-- how you are calling this? A little--

speaker: interviewer
text: Mound.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mound. A mound of earth. And here that was [INAUDIBLE]. And the owner from that part came to me and tell me, listen, you haven't got what to look after here.

speaker: interviewer
text: You can see, and he mentioned that to me, that a month ago, two months ago, I don't remember what he said, but a short time ago, before the Radom was liberated through the Russian army, a Sondercommando from Jews under the-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] and on top, who was carrying them on? That will come to me. [? Mendelsson. ?] A chap, he was--

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. He was most probably he was-- he was the head of that commando that took out everyone, they were putting on a fence around that place, and they were burning the whole corpse, and everything was-- so I listened to him, what he told me, and that was the end of it. But the graves from the [INAUDIBLE], from the Russian prisoners of war, were still existing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's just clarify something. The date you said that you were told your father was taken away was the third of May. What year was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was 1943. Actually, he was taken away in 1941. In-- sorry-- 194-- sorry, [INAUDIBLE]. In the first of May, '43. The first of May, '43.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know the names of any of the other people that perished with your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. There was another-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Bojmin.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: B-O-J-M-I-N. Was another-- Hendel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell that, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: H-E-N-D-E-L. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [SIGHS] Actually, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] they arrest 20 Jews that time, the first of May, '43, 1943. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Amongst them was Dr. Szendorowicz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell that, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Dr. Szendorowicz-- S-Z-E-N-D-O-- [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] R-O-W-I-C-Z. Szendorowicz. Dr. Szendorowicz. And was another one, Kormin.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: K-O-R-M-I-N. He was the son of a-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] he was the owner of a tannery. Schmel-- his father was [? Shmuel ?] Kormin-- [? Shmuel ?] Kormin.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they all perished?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. They all perished, no. That son didn't perish. He escaped when they took him from-- on the second May, to [INAUDIBLE], on a truck from the-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [INAUDIBLE] From the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not ghetto. It's not a ghetto. They were arrested and they put them in jail. From jail. [INAUDIBLE] From the jail, he escape, he-- from the truck. And I was talking to him in Lublin. He told me the whole story. That is 100% what I-- what I-- what I am telling you. But he escape. He escape. Szendorowicz was sent to Auschwitz. Dr. Szendorowicz was sent to Auschwitz so that from the 20 left, only 18 people, and amongst them, my father.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were told the story by the young policeman, and it was confirmed by Kormin.

speaker: interviewee
text: Was confirmed by that Kormin, that son of [? Shmuel ?] Kormin.

speaker: interviewer
text: After you found out about what happened to your father, did you return to Gdansk?

speaker: interviewee
text: I returned to Gdansk.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long did you stay there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Till illegally, when I left Gdansk, left Poland, through the town of [? Klatzko ?] with other Jews, illegally leaving Poland, going to, that time, to Palestine.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have false papers?

speaker: interviewee
text: No papers. It's not allowed. I got some papers done before, but by going through-- by going through the [? Klatzko, ?] the-- the border, Czechoslovakia-Poland, we should get rid of them. Some of them, actually, I have. That is-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] that is out-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] to become a Czechoslovakian-- Czechoslovakian citizen, I got this ill-- [? illegitimation. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this done through any organization, your planned trip to Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, to become-- to become-- to be able to get out a [INAUDIBLE] passport, [INAUDIBLE] that I am not-- that I am international-- an international passport.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who helped you [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] make your plans to go to Palestine?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't need nobody, because that was implanted in me through my dear father, so--

speaker: interviewer
text: What I mean is, practically, was there an organization that assisted you?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was an organization, too, Yichud. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of the organization?

speaker: interviewee
text: The organization was Yichud. Yichud, Yichud. Zusammen. Was waiting for the-- and the Bricha, what was working there.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're going to take a break now and just change tapes.
